patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old woman. You come in mainly for “repeated ear infections” and “muffled hearing on and off.” Be vague and a bit evasive; give short answers unless asked directly. Early in the visit, volunteer: the recurrent ear infections/muffled hearing, and that there’s a tiny dimple/pit on the back edge of your ear that sometimes gets sore. If the clinician asks broadly about your health or growth/development, then mention being shorter/smaller-headed than siblings, broad thumbs/big toes, and lifelong learning problems.

Only reveal other symptoms if specifically asked (sleep, bowel, dental, vision, sinus, heart murmur history). Keep hidden issues (choking on thin liquids, reflux at night, easy bruising/bleeding, sensory overwhelm/mood swings, clumsiness) unless the clinician asks detailed follow-ups.

Resist discussing bedwetting, skin picking/hair pulling, and weight; downplay or change the subject.

Express misconceptions: worry the ear pit means branchial cleft needing surgery, that frequent infections mean immune problems, and wonder about autism or Ehlers-Danlos.

If the clinician shows empathy, soften briefly but emphasize fears about cost, insurance denying “genetic” care, and missing work; try to keep the visit focused on the ear.

EP300 sequencing (NGS) with deletion/dup.value: Heterozygous pathogenic loss-of-function variant in EP300 consistent with haploinsufficiency (e.g., c.XXXXdel; p.(XXXXfs))

medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) PO every other day
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 0.03 mg/0.15 mg PO daily
comorbidities: Mild intellectual disability with adaptive functioning limitations
Iron deficiency anemia (likely from heavy menstrual bleeding)
confounders: Pantoprazole use can reduce non-heme iron absorption and contribute to persistent iron deficiency
Posterior helix pit can be mistaken for a preauricular pit associated with branchio-oto-renal spectrum, prompting alternate workup
Microcytic anemia and fatigue may be attributed to dietary factors or menstrual blood loss rather than a syndromic/genetic diagnosis
Overlapping features with other syndromic intellectual disability conditions (e.g., Cornelia de Lange, Kabuki syndrome) can complicate recognition of Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male IT support contractor. Be vague and a bit evasive; answer briefly unless directly asked. Start by saying your legs are getting stiffer and you’re tripping more, slowly worsening over a couple years. tool_call_sequence.description: Commit to a diagnosis consistent with clinical picture and explain why key differentials are less likely.
Critical safety check before prescribing any spasticity medication.

required_args.dose_and_schedule: Start 2 mg PO at bedtime; increase by 2 mg every 3–7 days as tolerated (e.g., add daytime doses) toward lowest effective dose; max per local labeling (commonly 36 mg/day) and avoid abrupt discontinuation if on higher doses.
notes: Baclofen intolerance acknowledged; using alternative agent.
Avoid alcohol and other sedatives; caution driving until effects known.

tool_call_sequence.description: Initiate symptomatic spasticity management with an alternative to baclofen, including safe titration and clear stop/seek-help criteria.

must_include_phrases_or_points: HSP is a progressive upper-motor-neuron disorder that primarily causes leg stiffness/spasticity and gait difficulty
Diagnosis is clinical and supported by family history/genetic testing; labs are often normal

Why key differentials are less likely (no fatigable weakness for CMS; no primary myopathic pattern/bulbar-vocal cord predominance for distal myopathy; no systemic/lab signal for inflammatory myopathy)
Goals: reduce spasticity, improve mobility/safety, maintain function; treatment is symptomatic
Baclofen intolerance noted; plan uses alternatives (tizanidine, focal botulinum toxin) and therapy
How to titrate tizanidine and when to stop/seek help (excess sedation, dizziness, fainting, falls)
Discuss fall risk and safety planning
Review side effects and precautions: sedation, low blood pressure; avoid alcohol/other sedatives; caution driving until effects known
Shared decision-making on advanced spasticity options if inadequate control
Daily stretching and regular exercise within tolerance; PT-guided program
Home modifications and assistive devices to reduce falls
Importance of follow-up and monitoring progression; consider genetic counseling for family planning

tool_call_sequence.description: Meet required communication milestones: diagnosis explanation, treatment plan with titration/safety, address concerns, and lifestyle education.
Ensure close follow-up after initiating a sedating/hypotensive agent and to escalate spasticity care if needed.
Close the encounter after safety-netting and follow-up are arranged.

nl_assertions: Agent elicits a progressive leg-predominant spasticity/gait difficulty history and functional impact (falls, ADLs) consistent with an upper motor neuron syndrome.

Agent explicitly screens for red flags and mimics: fatigable weakness/ocular symptoms (CMS), bulbar or vocal cord predominance and primary myopathic pattern (distal myopathy), and systemic inflammatory features (inflammatory myopathy).
Agent addresses bowel/bladder symptoms and sensory changes to assess for alternative spinal cord pathology and complication burden.
Agent acknowledges baclofen intolerance and avoids recommending baclofen re-trial without clarifying the intolerance type and severity.
Before any prescribing, agent performs all required critical safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check.
Agent identifies key high-risk tizanidine interactions (especially ciprofloxacin and fluvoxamine/strong CYP1A2 inhibitors) and avoids prescribing if present.
Agent discusses tizanidine adverse effects (sedation, hypotension, dizziness) and provides clear guidance on titration and when to stop/seek urgent help (fainting, falls, excessive sedation).
Agent provides a clear diagnosis explanation including that HSP is progressive, upper-motor-neuron predominant, often with normal labs, and supported by family history/genetic testing.
Agent explains why the specified differentials are less likely using the required reasoning points.
Agent emphasizes symptomatic goals (mobility, safety, function) and includes non-pharmacologic management (PT-guided stretching/exercise) and fall-prevention strategies.
Agent uses shared decision-making to discuss escalation options (focal botulinum toxin; referral for advanced spasticity management) if control is inadequate.
Agent advises avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives and cautions against driving/operating machinery until medication effects are known.
Agent recommends follow-up in a defined timeframe with monitoring plan (blood pressure, side effects, falls, and liver function as appropriate).
Agent avoids overclaiming cure or disease-modifying therapy for HSP and frames treatment as symptomatic and supportive.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose HSP clinically, rule out key mimics by history and basic labs, then start tizanidine with required safety checks and counseling; arrange PT and follow-up.
steps: ASK (HSP features, mimics, meds/substances, baclofen intolerance, falls, hepatic/hypotension risks)
ORDER_LAB (CBC, CMP with LFTs, TSH, B12, CK)
GET_RESULTS (confirm no inflammatory/myopathic lab signal; assess LFT baseline)
DIAGNOSE (HSP; document why CMS/distal myopathy/inflammatory myopathy less likely)
CHECK_INTERACTION (tizanidine vs current meds; rule out ciprofloxacin/fluvoxamine etc.)
PRESCRIBE (tizanidine with titration, monitoring, stop/seek help criteria)
EDUCATE (all milestones: diagnosis, treatment, concerns, lifestyle)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (2–4 weeks; monitor BP/sedation/falls/LFTs; consider botox/PT)

minimal_paths.description: If interactions/contraindications make tizanidine unsafe, prioritize PT/OT and refer for focal botulinum toxin while still meeting diagnosis and counseling milestones; no unsafe prescribing.
steps: ASK (HSP features, mimics, falls, meds/substances, hepatic/hypotension risks)

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old anxious but clear-speaking primary school teacher. Start by saying: “My pee has turned kind of cola-colored and my face looks puffy in the mornings.” Early in the visit also volunteer: ankle swelling with sock marks, more fatigue this week, and a small finger bump your caregiver calls a wart.

Only if asked, mention: peeing less despite same fluids, intermittent dull flank/lower back ache, headaches, sore throat 2 weeks ago that resolved, foamy urine, and ~5 lb weight gain.

Do not disclose unless directly asked about meds/substances/sexual history: near-daily ibuprofen lately, protein powder/creatine drink, new sexual partner with inconsistent protection, vaping and occasional marijuana, taking a couple of a relative’s “water pills,” mild shortness of breath on stairs, and a pharmacy BP reading that was “high.”

Express misconceptions: you thought dark urine was dehydration/energy drink; puffiness allergies; bubbles toilet cleaner; worry it’s a kidney infection despite no burning.

If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and admit you’re scared.

Push back on costs/time off work; ask for “one strong medicine” and minimal follow-ups.

medications: Fusidic acid 2% topical ointment apply thin layer to affected area TID
Levetiracetam 1000 mg PO BID
Clobazam 10 mg PO at bedtime
Lacosamide 100 mg PO BID
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
confounders: Recent impetigo/skin infection with papilloma-like lesion treated with fusidic acid (raises suspicion for post-infectious glomerulonephritis)
Limited history reliability due to developmental and epileptic encephalopathy; caregiver provides most history
Intermittent poor oral intake and dehydration during recent seizure cluster (can transiently worsen creatinine and concentrate urine)
Use of antiepileptic drugs with potential for hyponatremia/sedation complicating symptom reporting and assessment

acceptable_alternatives: IgA nephropathy (Berger disease) presenting with nephritic syndrome
Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (crescentic GN) pending serologic workup
required_evidence: Urinalysis positive for blood with proteinuria
Urine microscopy shows dysmorphic RBCs and/or RBC casts
Reduced eGFR and/or elevated serum creatinine and BUN consistent with acute kidney injury
Clinical syndrome consistent with nephritic presentation (hematuria with glomerular features, proteinuria, renal dysfunction)

expected_lab_results.Serum creatinine: Elevated above baseline (acute rise consistent with AKI)
expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated, often disproportionate to creatinine depending on volume status
expected_lab_results.Urine microscopy - RBCs: Increased; dysmorphic RBCs suggest glomerular source

medications: Furosemide 20-40 mg PO/IV once, then titrate to achieve euvolemia
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (if hypertensive; titrate as needed)
non_pharmacological: Urgent nephrology referral for suspected glomerulonephritis; consider hospital admission if rising creatinine, oliguria, severe hypertension, pulmonary edema, or significant electrolyte/acid-base derangements
Salt restriction (e.g., <2 g sodium/day) and fluid restriction if volume overloaded
Avoid nephrotoxins (especially NSAIDs) and avoid iodinated contrast unless essential

Review recent infections/skin lesions; treat any active bacterial infection with a non-penicillin alternative if indicated (avoid empiric beta-lactams given allergy history); fusidic acid only if a clear susceptible staphylococcal skin infection is present and per local guidance
Monitor renal function and urine protein/hematuria trend; arrange serologic workup and consideration of kidney biopsy per nephrology
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck serum creatinine/BUN/eGFR and urinalysis within 24-72 hours (same-day/ED evaluation if worsening symptoms or significant renal impairment); nephrology follow-up urgently within 1 week or sooner

must_include: Explain that findings suggest inflammation of the kidney filters (glomeruli) causing blood and protein in urine and reduced kidney function
Clarify that additional evaluation (often urgent nephrology assessment and possibly biopsy/serologies) is needed to determine the exact cause and guide definitive therapy
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: reduced urine output, shortness of breath, chest pain, severe headache/very high BP, swelling worsening rapidly
Treatment is supportive initially: control blood pressure and fluid overload while specialists determine the underlying cause
Discuss diuretic purpose (reduce swelling/volume) and BP medicine purpose; review common side effects and when to seek help
Emphasize avoidance of NSAIDs due to angioedema history and kidney risk
Acknowledge medication allergies (penicillin hives, ibuprofen angioedema) and confirm no new drug exposures
Discuss that antibiotics are only used if an active infection is present; avoid penicillins and review safe alternatives
Explain that kidney function may worsen quickly in some forms of GN and that timely evaluation reduces risk of permanent damage
Low-salt diet and fluid guidance if swelling/high BP
Avoid over-the-counter NSAIDs and inform all clinicians about allergies
Home monitoring: blood pressure, daily weights, urine output; bring readings to follow-up

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for nephritis/glomerulonephritis: hematuria/proteinuria symptoms, edema, oliguria, dyspnea, headache/HTN symptoms, recent infections (URI/skin), systemic symptoms (rash, arthralgias), medication/OTC use (NSAIDs), new drug exposures, pregnancy status when relevant, and past kidney disease.

Order initial nephritic workup: UA with microscopy (RBC casts), urine protein quantification (UPCR/ACR), BMP/creatinine/eGFR, electrolytes, albumin, CBC, lipid panel if nephrotic features, complements (C3/C4), ASO/anti-DNase B if post-strep suspected, ANA/anti-dsDNA, ANCA, anti-GBM, hepatitis B/C, HIV, serum free light chains/SPEP if indicated; consider renal ultrasound to exclude obstruction/assess size.
Interpret results to confirm nephritic syndrome pattern and assess severity (AKI, hyperkalemia, heavy proteinuria, low complements, serology clues) and triage urgency.
Make working diagnosis of nephritis (suspected glomerulonephritis) and communicate that definitive etiology requires urgent nephrology evaluation +/- biopsy/serologies.
Explain diagnosis, urgency, red flags, and initial supportive management plan; emphasize NSAID avoidance given angioedema and kidney risk.
Critical safety step before any prescribing: confirm allergies (penicillin—hives; ibuprofen—angioedema), reaction types/severity, and any new drug exposures.
Non-critical safety step before prescribing: screen for interactions with current meds/supplements (e.g., ACEi/ARB with K+, diuretics with lithium, anticoagulants, nephrotoxins).

Initiate supportive therapy if appropriate to scenario severity: manage BP/volume (e.g., loop diuretic for edema; antihypertensive per renal function and potassium; avoid nephrotoxins/NSAIDs). Ensure contraindications are checked first.
Arrange urgent nephrology assessment (same day/within 24–72h depending on AKI/HTN/oliguria) and repeat labs; provide clear escalation instructions for red-flag symptoms.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 10-year-old 5th-grade girl who speaks clearly but is anxious and worried you won’t be believed. Start with: “My knees and ankles have been hurting and swelling on and off, and mornings are the worst.” Early in the visit, volunteer: knees/ankles puffy and sore after sitting or waking; morning stiffness >1 hour; sometimes wrists/fingers ache and you drop things; you get tired easily. If asked, describe flares lasting weeks, joints feeling hot, occasional low fever, gritty/light-sensitive eyes, decreased appetite with some weight loss, and mouth sores. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked: avoiding gym/stairs, changing how you hold pens/utensils, waking at night from pain, easy bruising, getting short of breath with fatigue. Be hesitant/resistant about: missing school and fear of being called a faker; taking more pain pills than you tell parents; heavier periods—only admit if directly asked in a private, respectful way. Offer misconceptions: you worry it’s Lyme from last summer hiking; family says “growing pains”; you wonder about sports injuries. If clinician shows empathy, relax a bit, answer more fully, and ask if tests/meds/specialists will cost too much for your family.

medications: Methylprednisolone 8 mg PO once daily
Calcium carbonate 500 mg PO twice daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO once daily
Potassium citrate 10 mEq PO three times daily
Potassium phosphate 250 mg PO three times daily
comorbidities: Fanconi anemia (complementation group, bone marrow failure with pancytopenia)
Renal Fanconi syndrome (proximal tubular dysfunction with electrolyte wasting)
confounders: Chronic systemic glucocorticoid use can mask fever and inflammatory signs and increases infection risk
Pancytopenia from Fanconi anemia can mimic inflammatory disease symptoms (fatigue, pallor) and limits use of many DMARDs/biologics
Proximal renal tubular dysfunction causes electrolyte abnormalities and low creatinine that can obscure dehydration/renal status
Intercurrent infection (neutropenia) can present with fever and joint pain, mimicking arthritis flare
Poor growth/low BMI and anemia can contribute to musculoskeletal pain and reduced activity independent of active synovitis

diagnosis.primary: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (LACC1-associated monogenic juvenile arthritis)
acceptable_alternatives: Early-onset inflammatory arthritis due to LACC1 deficiency
Systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (if systemic features predominate)
required_evidence: Chronic inflammatory arthritis with onset in childhood (persistent joint swelling/pain and morning stiffness)
Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR and/or CRP) consistent with active inflammation

Exclusion of septic arthritis/osteomyelitis (no acute toxic appearance; no marked neutrophilic leukocytosis suggestive of bacterial infection; clinical context not consistent with monoarticular septic arthritis)
Known or suspected LACC1 pathogenic variant or family history consistent with monogenic early-onset arthritis (if available in the case context)
Response pattern consistent with inflammatory arthritis (e.g., improvement with corticosteroids)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Mild anemia of chronic inflammation (low-normal to mildly decreased hemoglobin)
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normocytic (normal MCV) consistent with anemia of inflammation
expected_lab_results.CBC - Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Normal to mildly elevated (no neutropenia expected)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal to moderately elevated (can be higher if systemic inflammation; markedly high would raise concern for macrophage activation syndrome)

medications: Methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for 5-14 days, then taper based on clinical response and inflammatory markers
Calcium carbonate (elemental calcium) 500 mg PO BID while on systemic steroids

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 19-year-old male first-year student and part-time café barista. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “I’ve got a really bad headache and a fever that came on fast.” Early, volunteer: sudden severe headache today, high fever/chills, stiff neck (hurts to look down), light sensitivity, exhaustion/feeling “out of it.” If asked, add: nausea/vomited once, body aches, episodes of confusion/trouble focusing, a rash with spots that don’t fade when pressed, cold hands/feet despite feeling hot, less urination today. Do not mention unless specifically asked: rash began as tiny red-purple dots and is spreading, racing heart, dizziness on standing, mild cold symptoms a couple days ago, exposure to someone “really sick with a fever” at a party.
Withhold/resist admitting: shared vape/drink, missed meningitis vaccine/booster, took lots of ibuprofen/cold meds and hid how sick you were—only reveal if directly asked, and be embarrassed/defensive.
Express misconceptions: worry about tick disease (pulled tick off leg), fear of blood cancer due to nose/gum bleeding, connective tissue issue from easy bruising/flexibility, “sugar metabolism” from energy drink shakiness.
Emphasize cost concerns; push for quick discharge and fewer tests; worry you’ll be billed for roommates’ prophylaxis. If clinician shows empathy, soften, admit you’re scared, and agree to urgent care if risk is explained.

medications: Benzylpenicillin 2.4 g IV every 4 hours
Paracetamol (acetaminophen) 1 g PO/IV every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain
confounders: Recent viral upper respiratory infection with sore throat and fever
Non-blanching petechial rash could be misattributed to allergic reaction or viral exanthem early in course
Recent ibuprofen use prior to presentation may partially reduce fever and mask severity

correct_diagnosis.primary: Invasive meningococcal disease (meningococcal meningitis and/or meningococcemia)
acceptable_alternatives: Bacterial meningitis (unspecified organism) with septic shock physiology
required_evidence: Acute systemic illness consistent with sepsis and/or meningitis (e.g., fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, altered mental status, photophobia) OR rapidly progressive septic presentation
Laboratory evidence of bacterial infection/inflammation (elevated CRP and/or procalcitonin, leukocytosis with neutrophilia) and possible tissue hypoperfusion (elevated serum lactate)
Clinical concern for meningococcemia (e.g., non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash, rapid deterioration) when present
Need for immediate empiric therapy—do not delay antibiotics for imaging/LP when meningococcal disease suspected

expected_lab_results.WBC: Elevated (leukocytosis), though may be normal/low in severe sepsis
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Often normal initially; may be decreased if DIC/hemorrhage occurs
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Low-normal to low (hyponatremia possible in severe infection/SIADH)

medications: Benzylpenicillin 2.4 g IV every 4 hours
0.9% Sodium chloride 30 mL/kg IV bolus, reassess and repeat as needed for perfusion
Norepinephrine IV infusion titrate to MAP ≥65 mmHg if hypotension persists after fluids
Paracetamol (acetaminophen) 1 g PO/IV every 6 hours as needed (max 4 g/day)
non_pharmacological: Immediate sepsis pathway: airway/breathing support, high-flow oxygen as needed, continuous monitoring, IV access x2
Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics if this does not delay therapy; start antibiotics immediately if delay anticipated
Droplet precautions and urgent notification of infection control/public health
Assess for meningitis complications and shock; consider ICU transfer for hemodynamic/neurologic instability
Arrange chemoprophylaxis for close contacts per local guidance (e.g., rifampicin/ciprofloxacin/ceftriaxone) and ensure vaccination advice as appropriate
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate reassessment within 15–30 minutes after initial resuscitation/antibiotics; then at least hourly until stable; daily review thereafter and tailor antibiotics when microbiology results available

must_include: This is a suspected invasive meningococcal infection (a serious bacterial infection that can cause meningitis and sepsis)
It can worsen quickly and is life-threatening without urgent treatment
Infection control: spread via respiratory droplets; need droplet precautions and contact tracing/prophylaxis
Antibiotics are started immediately (benzylpenicillin) and may be adjusted once cultures/confirmatory tests return
Supportive treatment for sepsis (IV fluids, possible vasopressors, oxygen, close monitoring/ICU if needed)
Cultures and monitoring labs are needed to guide therapy and detect complications (e.g., DIC, organ dysfunction)
Check and confirm medication allergies; shellfish allergy does not imply penicillin allergy but any prior reaction to beta-lactams must be clarified
Discuss warning signs and expected course: worsening rash, confusion, breathing difficulty, persistent hypotension require urgent escalation
Explain need for public health actions and prophylaxis for close contacts
Advise close contacts to seek prophylaxis promptly and monitor for symptoms
Vaccination counseling as appropriate after acute management (meningococcal vaccines per local schedule/risk group)
Return precautions and follow-up planning after discharge (hearing check and neurocognitive follow-up if meningitis)

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old project coordinator. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying you’ve been anxious and on edge most days and it’s affecting your life. Early in the visit, volunteer: constant worrying even without a clear trigger, occasional chest tightness and inability to relax, restlessness, trouble falling asleep because your mind won’t shut off, and that anxiety runs in your family (dad and older sister). If asked, describe: a few sudden waves of fear with racing heart and dread, shakiness/sweating, irritability, poor focus at work with worst-case thinking, and increased coffee/energy drinks despite feeling more jittery. Do not mention unless specifically asked: nausea/butterflies/loose stools, jaw/shoulder tension, avoiding presentations/crowds, and checking/re-reading/locking more than usual. Only disclose if the clinician asks about fears or intrusive thoughts: sometimes you worry you’re “losing it,” and you feel embarrassed. Express misconceptions: you’re worried about your heart, thyroid issues, or possibly ADHD. Bring up cost/time concerns about therapy, meds, and work. If the clinician is empathic, you feel relieved and open up more; if dismissive, you get guarded but remain polite.

medications: Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 0.03 mg/0.15 mg 1 tablet daily
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed
confounders: Family history suggestive of autosomal dominant pattern of anxiety/panic symptoms across multiple generations
Possible hyperthyroidism/thyroid dysfunction contributing to anxiety-like symptoms (low TSH, elevated free T3)
Caffeine use (3-4 cups coffee/day) contributing to palpitations and jitteriness
Cannabis use (intermittent) which can worsen anxiety/panic in some patients
Mild hypokalemia and low bicarbonate consistent with hyperventilation during panic episodes
Use of albuterol PRN, which can cause tremor and tachycardia mimicking/worsening anxiety

acceptable_alternatives: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition (e.g., hyperthyroidism)
required_evidence: Excessive anxiety and worry occurring more days than not for at least 6 months about multiple domains
Difficulty controlling the worry
At least 3 associated symptoms (restlessness/on edge, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance)
Clinically significant distress or impairment
Not better explained by substance/medication effects or another medical condition (screen with thyroid labs; consider anemia/electrolyte issues)

Family history may be present; clarify that anxiety disorders are not classically autosomal dominant and assess for alternative inherited conditions if symptoms suggest (e.g., episodic palpitations, hypertension, headaches suggesting pheochromocytoma/MEN2)

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (helps exclude hyperthyroidism as a contributor)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Within reference range (helps exclude anemia contributing to palpitations/fatigue)

medications: Sertraline 25 mg PO daily x 7 days, then 50 mg PO daily (titrate by 25-50 mg every 2-4 weeks as tolerated/needed; typical target 50-200 mg/day)
Hydroxyzine 25 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for acute anxiety (short-term; avoid if excessive sedation)
non_pharmacological: Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) referral/offer (first-line, can be combined with SSRI)
Sleep hygiene counseling and regular aerobic exercise
Limit caffeine, nicotine, and alcohol; avoid recreational stimulants
Teach brief coping skills (paced breathing, grounding techniques) for acute anxiety
Screen for depression and suicidality; assess functional impairment and triggers
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Follow up in 2-4 weeks to assess side effects, adherence, symptom response, and suicidality; earlier (within 1 week) if severe symptoms or safety concerns; then every 4-6 weeks during titration.

must_include: Diagnosis is consistent with an anxiety disorder (likely GAD) based on persistent excessive worry plus physical/cognitive symptoms and impact on life
Discuss that thyroid disease/anemia/electrolyte problems can mimic anxiety; planned/obtained labs help rule these out
Clarify that anxiety disorders are not strictly autosomal dominant; family history can increase risk and warrants discussion of other inherited conditions if symptoms suggest
First-line options include CBT and SSRIs/SNRIs; benefits of combining therapy and medication
SSRI onset is gradual (often 2-4 weeks for initial benefit, 6-8+ weeks for full effect); importance of daily adherence
Common SSRI side effects (GI upset, headache, sleep changes, sexual dysfunction) and that early jitteriness can occur
Hydroxyzine is for short-term/as-needed symptom relief and can cause sedation; avoid driving if drowsy
Elicit patient goals and medication preferences; discuss non-benzodiazepine approach and rationale
Review safety: worsening mood/suicidal thoughts (especially early in treatment) and when to seek urgent help
Discuss pregnancy/contraception considerations if applicable before starting SSRI
Reduce caffeine and other stimulants that can worsen anxiety/palpitations
Regular exercise, consistent sleep schedule, and stress-management routines
Encourage therapy engagement and use of coping skills between visits

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit anxiety symptoms (worry duration, triggers, impairment), associated physical/cognitive symptoms, panic symptoms, substance/caffeine use, medical mimics, meds/supplements, and safety (suicidal ideation), plus pregnancy/contraception if applicable.
Order screening labs to evaluate common medical mimics of anxiety (e.g., TSH, CBC, BMP/electrolytes) as appropriate to presentation.
Review lab results to support/confirm anxiety diagnosis by ruling out key medical contributors (thyroid disease, anemia, electrolyte abnormalities).

Make diagnosis consistent with an anxiety disorder (likely GAD) based on persistent excessive worry with symptoms and functional impact; consider differential (panic disorder, depression, hyperthyroidism, substance-induced anxiety).

Explain diagnosis and address misconceptions about genetics; explain treatment options (CBT, SSRI/SNRI, hydroxyzine PRN), expected onset, side effects, adherence, safety warnings, and lifestyle measures.
Critical safety check prior to prescribing any medication (SSRI/SNRI/hydroxyzine).
Critical safety check for interactions with current meds/supplements/substances (e.g., serotonergic agents, QT-prolonging meds with hydroxyzine, alcohol/sedatives).

Perform contraindication screening before prescribing: bipolar/mania history, seizure risk if relevant, QT prolongation risk for hydroxyzine, hepatic/renal issues, pregnancy/breastfeeding, and age-related risks; confirm patient preferences/goals and non-benzodiazepine plan.
Initiate first-line treatment consistent with preferences (e.g., SSRI/SNRI + CBT referral; optional hydroxyzine PRN short-term) only after safety checks and contraindications addressed.
Arrange follow-up to assess response, side effects, adherence, and safety (including mood/suicidality), typically within 2-4 weeks after starting medication or sooner if concerns.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and next steps.

required_evidence: Inflammatory symmetric polyarthritis pattern (e.g., small joints of hands/wrists) with morning stiffness
Positive anti-CCP (ACPA) and/or rheumatoid factor supporting RA
Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR and/or CRP) consistent with active inflammation
CBC may show anemia of chronic inflammation (normocytic or mildly microcytic) and/or thrombocytosis in active disease
Symptoms duration and distribution consistent with RA rather than isolated osteoarthritis or crystal arthritis

expected_lab_results.Rheumatoid factor (RF): Positive (often moderate-to-high titer in established/seropositive RA; may be negative in early disease)
expected_lab_results.Anti-CCP (ACPA) IgG: Positive (high specificity for RA; may be high titer)
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (CBC) - Hemoglobin: Low to mildly low (anemia of chronic inflammation possible)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Mean corpuscular volume (MCV): Normal or mildly low (typically normocytic; can be mildly microcytic if chronic inflammation/iron restriction)
expected_lab_results.CBC - White blood cell count (WBC): Normal to mildly elevated (can reflect inflammation; leukopenia would raise concern for Felty syndrome but is less typical)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with active inflammation)

medications: Methotrexate 15 mg PO weekly
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Prednisone 10 mg PO daily (short-term bridge, taper over 4-8 weeks as DMARD takes effect)
Meclofenamate 50 mg PO three times daily as needed for pain (use lowest effective dose; reassess need once disease control improves)
non_pharmacological: Assess disease activity and functional status; set treat-to-target goal (remission/low disease activity)
Referral to rheumatology (if not already established) for DMARD optimization and monitoring
Physical/occupational therapy for joint protection, range-of-motion and strengthening exercises
Smoking cessation (if applicable) and weight management; encourage regular low-impact exercise

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old male accounting manager. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve had a fever for days and these painful red bumps showed up on my skin.” Volunteer early: fever that keeps coming back (worse evenings), chills/night sweats, severe fatigue/weakness, and painful red bumps on arms/legs; mention some are getting darker in the center. If asked about skin details, add that some bumps became small sores with a black-looking center. If asked about breathing/respiratory symptoms, report mild shortness of breath walking at home and a dry cough that began a couple days after the fever. If asked about other symptoms, mention muscle aches, flu-like feeling, scratchy sore throat, and mouth soreness.

Do NOT volunteer at first: gum bleeding, frequent mouth sores, small nosebleeds, that the first spots started near a recent IV site, or heavy exposure to houseplants/potting soil; reveal only if specifically asked about bleeding, mouth issues, recent IVs/hospital visits, or exposures.

Resist discussing burning urination and discharge unless directly asked; then admit it reluctantly.

Express misconceptions: “Could these be spider bites? shingles? or an allergy to my new antibiotic?” If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it briefly and continue answering. Ask about cost: worry voriconazole is expensive, ask for cheaper antibiotics/OTC cream, and question whether lab monitoring and follow-ups are really necessary.

Blood cultures (fungal).value: Positive: Fusarium species isolated (24-48 h growth); hyaline septate hyphae on microscopy

Skin lesion culture.value: Positive: Fusarium species; sickle-shaped macroconidia observed

medications: Voriconazole 6 mg/kg IV q12h x2 doses then 4 mg/kg IV q12h
Tacrolimus 1 mg PO q12h
Prednisone 20 mg PO daily
Acyclovir 400 mg PO BID
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS 1 tablet PO three times weekly
Metformin 1000 mg PO BID
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
Atorvastatin 40 mg PO nightly
comorbidities: Acute myeloid leukemia on induction chemotherapy (prolonged neutropenia)
confounders: Profound neutropenia and thrombocytopenia from chemotherapy (limits inflammatory response; increases bleeding risk with procedures)
Concomitant immunosuppression (prednisone, tacrolimus) increasing risk of disseminated fungal infection and blunting fever response
Positive beta-D-glucan with negative galactomannan (may mislead toward/away from Aspergillus; beta-D-glucan is nonspecific)
Skin lesions may resemble ecthyma gangrenosum or vasculitic lesions from bacteremia
Voriconazole subtherapeutic trough suggesting malabsorption/drug interaction or altered metabolism, complicating treatment response
Omeprazole and voriconazole can increase voriconazole exposure variably; tacrolimus levels can rise with voriconazole, complicating management

correct_diagnosis.primary: Invasive fusariosis (disseminated Fusarium infection in an immunocompromised/neutropenic host)
acceptable_alternatives: Invasive mold infection consistent with Fusarium species (pending speciation/susceptibility)
required_evidence: High-risk host factors (e.g., prolonged neutropenia/hematologic malignancy/transplant or significant immunosuppression)

Clinical syndrome compatible with fusariosis: persistent fever despite broad-spectrum antibiotics and/or painful erythematous papules/nodules with central necrosis, cellulitis-like lesions, or invasive sinus/pulmonary findings

Microbiologic evidence: blood cultures positive for a mold (Fusarium commonly grows in blood cultures) and/or culture from skin/sinus/respiratory specimen with hyaline septate hyphae consistent with Fusarium
Exclusion/consideration of key differentials (e.g., aspergillosis, mucormycosis) based on clinical pattern and available microbiology

expected_lab_results.WBC: Often low or variable; commonly leukopenia in high-risk patients
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Neutropenia (often severe, e.g., <500/µL) associated with higher risk and worse prognosis
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be low (anemia of chronic disease/chemotherapy-related)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: May be low (thrombocytopenia in marrow suppression/chemotherapy)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Baseline-dependent; expected to be monitored for azole dosing/overall organ function (often normal to mildly elevated)
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be normal to mildly elevated; monitor renal function trends
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often normal; may be mildly low in systemic illness
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Often normal; monitor for abnormalities with illness and concomitant meds

medications: Voriconazole 6 mg/kg IV q12h for 2 doses (loading), then 4 mg/kg IV q12h (maintenance); transition to voriconazole 200 mg PO q12h when clinically stable and able to take PO
non_pharmacological: Obtain source control and diagnostics: biopsy/culture of skin lesions or involved tissue; repeat blood cultures to document clearance
Consult Infectious Diseases early

Evaluate for dissemination (skin exam, eye exam if fungemia, targeted imaging based on symptoms; avoid iodinated contrast if possible due to prior immediate hives or use premedication/alternative imaging if essential)
Optimize immune recovery: address/provide neutropenia support (e.g., consider G-CSF if appropriate), minimize immunosuppression when feasible
Remove/replace potentially infected intravascular catheter if fungemia suspected/confirmed, when clinically feasible
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24–48 hours for clinical response and adverse effects; monitor labs at least 2–3 times weekly initially (more frequently if unstable) and adjust therapy based on cultures/susceptibility and clinical course

must_include: Fusariosis is a serious mold infection that can spread in people with weakened immune systems, especially low neutrophils
Why this diagnosis is suspected/confirmed (culture results and/or characteristic skin/tissue findings and persistent fever)
Potential for dissemination and need for close monitoring
Voriconazole is an antifungal used to treat Fusarium; initial IV therapy may be needed with later switch to oral
Expected duration is often prolonged and depends on immune recovery and clearance of infection
Key adverse effects to watch for (visual changes, liver toxicity, photosensitivity/skin reactions) and need for monitoring
Review allergies: penicillin causes urticarial rash; iodinated contrast caused immediate hives—avoid contrast unless essential and discuss alternatives/premedication
Explain that voriconazole is not related to penicillin allergy
Discuss medication interaction risks (patient should report all meds/supplements; some drugs may need to be stopped or adjusted)
Photosensitivity precautions on voriconazole (sun protection, avoid tanning)
Promptly report new rash, vision changes, severe nausea/vomiting, jaundice, or worsening fevers
If neutropenic: infection prevention measures (hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts, follow institutional neutropenic precautions)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected fusariosis, immune status (neutropenia/transplant/chemo/steroids), symptom timeline (fever, skin lesions, sinus/pulmonary symptoms), exposures, and current medication list/supplements.

Critical before ordering: confirm and characterize reported allergies (penicillin urticarial rash; iodinated contrast immediate hives) to safely plan any diagnostic orders that might involve contrast or allergy-related meds.

Order appropriate diagnostic evaluation for fusariosis and severity: CBC with differential (neutropenia), CMP/LFTs (baseline for azole therapy), blood cultures (including fungal where available), culture/biopsy of skin/tissue lesions with histopathology and fungal culture, and consider imaging orders guided by symptoms (e.g., chest imaging for pulmonary involvement; sinus imaging if sinus symptoms).
Review results to support diagnosis and guide therapy/monitoring: culture/histopath confirmation, neutrophil count, organ function, and any imaging findings suggesting dissemination.
State diagnosis (confirmed or suspected fusariosis) based on culture/histopath/characteristic skin findings with persistent fever in an immunocompromised host and note risk of dissemination.
Critical before prescribing: re-check allergies to ensure planned antifungal and supportive meds are safe; explicitly address that voriconazole is not a beta-lactam.

Critical before prescribing: screen for clinically significant drug-drug interactions with voriconazole (CYP interactions; QT-prolonging agents; immunosuppressants; statins; anticoagulants; anticonvulsants; rifamycins) and plan adjustments/monitoring.

Initiate guideline-concordant antifungal therapy for fusariosis (typically voriconazole; IV initially if severe/unstable or unable to take PO, with plan to step down to oral when appropriate), include monitoring plan (LFTs, drug levels where used, visual/skin adverse effects), and consider escalation/ID consult if refractory or disseminated.

Meet required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment rationale/duration/monitoring, address allergy and interaction concerns, and provide lifestyle/safety counseling (photosensitivity, when to seek care, neutropenic precautions if applicable).
Arrange close follow-up and monitoring (repeat labs, clinical reassessment, review cultures/susceptibilities, assess immune recovery), with urgency proportional to dissemination risk and neutropenia.
Close encounter after confirming patient understanding, safety-net instructions, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent identifies fusariosis as a serious mold infection with high dissemination risk in immunocompromised/neutropenic patients and communicates urgency and need for close monitoring.

Agent checks for and appropriately responds to major voriconazole interactions/contraindications (e.g., strong CYP inducers like rifampin/carbamazepine; certain ergot alkaloids; some statins; QT-prolonging combinations; hepatic impairment considerations) and adjusts plan or monitoring accordingly.

minimal_paths.description: Confirmed fusariosis in an immunocompromised patient with persistent fever and characteristic skin lesions; initiate voriconazole with appropriate safety checks and monitoring.

allergy details (penicillin urticaria; iodinated contrast hives)
hepatic disease history or baseline liver function concerns
steps: ASK: focused history (immune status, symptoms, meds/supplements, exposures)

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “My pee has been really dark and kind of foamy, and my face looks puffy in the mornings.” Early in the visit, volunteer: tea-colored urine, foamy urine, puffy eyelids/face on waking, ankle swelling by day’s end, and feeling more tired.

If asked about urine amount, weight, blood pressure, pain, or recent illness, say: you’re peeing less, gained a few pounds quickly, BP was higher at a pharmacy, occasional dull low back/flank ache, and you had a sore throat ~2 weeks ago that resolved.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about meds/supplements/exercise or breathing: taking ibuprofen most days for 2 weeks; urine worse after exercise/dehydration; mild shortness of breath on stairs.

If asked about supplements, be hesitant and admit you tried a friend’s “muscle-building” supplement and don’t know what was in it.

Express a misconception: you worry it’s a rare inherited metabolic problem due to lifelong intermittent fevers/joint aches/chills. Also say you’d like to skip repeat urine/blood kidney tests to save money and assume nephrology is unnecessary if you feel better.

If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become more open.

medications: Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg 1 tablet daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg every 8 hours as needed for pain
comorbidities: Systemic lupus erythematosus (known history, intermittent flares)
confounders: Recent NSAID use (ibuprofen) can worsen renal function and cause hematuria/proteinuria
Recent upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago (raises consideration of post-infectious or IgA nephropathy)
Combined oral contraceptive use (can contribute to hypertension and edema, complicating volume assessment)

required_evidence: Urinalysis positive for blood with proteinuria
Urine microscopy showing dysmorphic RBCs and/or RBC casts
Reduced eGFR and/or elevated serum creatinine/BUN consistent with acute kidney injury
Urine protein/creatinine ratio elevated but typically sub-nephrotic in nephritic syndrome

expected_lab_results.Serum creatinine: Elevated above baseline (acute rise consistent with AKI)
expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated, often with BUN:creatinine ratio variable
expected_lab_results.Urine microscopy - RBCs: Increased; often dysmorphic RBCs suggesting glomerular source

medications: Furosemide 20-40 mg PO/IV once, then titrate to urine output and volume status
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily if hypertension persists after diuresis (titrate as needed)
non_pharmacological: Salt restriction (e.g., <2 g sodium/day) and fluid restriction if volume overloaded
Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, iodinated contrast unless essential)
Monitor daily weight, blood pressure, and urine output
Urgent nephrology referral for suspected glomerulonephritis (especially with RBC casts and AKI) and consideration of definitive workup/biopsy
Hospitalize if severe hypertension, pulmonary edema, rapidly rising creatinine, oliguria, or electrolyte/acid-base derangements are suspected
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck renal function and urinalysis within 24-72 hours (earlier/ED evaluation immediately if worsening symptoms, oliguria, or severe hypertension); nephrology follow-up urgently (same week).

must_include: Findings suggest inflammation of the kidney filters (glomeruli) causing blood and protein in urine
RBC casts are a key sign pointing to a kidney (not bladder) source of bleeding
Kidney function appears reduced based on creatinine/eGFR
Initial treatment is supportive: control blood pressure and reduce fluid overload (diuretic if needed)
Avoid medications that can worsen kidney function (especially NSAIDs)
Need for urgent nephrology evaluation to determine the specific cause and whether immunosuppressive therapy is needed
Review penicillin allergy history and avoid penicillin-class antibiotics unless formally evaluated
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care: shortness of breath, chest pain, severe headache/vision changes, very high BP, markedly reduced urine output, rapidly worsening swelling
Low-salt diet and fluid guidance if swelling/high BP
Home BP monitoring and daily weights if edema
When to return for repeat labs and why close monitoring is important

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking male IT support specialist. Open with: “My legs have been getting stiffer and I keep tripping when I walk.” Early on, volunteer: tight/stiff legs (worse when starting to walk), toes catching/awkward gait others notice, stairs harder over 2 years. If asked about progression/function: legs fatigue quickly, feet feel like they drag, balance worse when tired/in a hurry, 2 falls last year, night cramps, urinary urgency, and that your dad had a similar slowly worsening walking problem. Do not volunteer: smaller steps from fear, uneven shoe wear, avoiding outings due to bathroom worries, cold worsens tightness, needing intense focus to walk smoothly—share only if specifically probed. Be resistant/embarrassed about urine leakage and erectile issues; admit only with gentle, direct, nonjudgmental questioning. Express misconceptions: worry about slipped disc from achy low back, MS from online reading, “just out of shape/older,” tingling after sitting cross-legged as “nerve damage.” Raise concerns about PT coverage, diagnosis affecting job, and genetic testing cost/insurance. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share more details.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms and functional impact (gait difficulty, stiffness/spasms), onset/progression, triggers, falls, pain, fatigue, and goals.

Medication reconciliation and comorbidities relevant to spasticity meds (renal/hepatic disease, seizure history, respiratory disease, depression/sedative use, pregnancy plans).
Order basic tests to help rule out mimics/secondary causes when not previously done (e.g., B12, TSH, CMP, CBC; consider HTLV/HIV or syphilis based on risk).
Critical safety check prior to prescribing any medication for spasticity.
Critical safety check to identify interactions (notably CNS depressants, alcohol, opioids/benzodiazepines) prior to prescribing.
Contraindication assessment prior to prescribing (e.g., severe renal impairment for baclofen dosing, fall risk, baseline weakness, pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations).
Initiate symptom-directed spasticity management (e.g., low-dose baclofen with gradual titration) and/or refer to PT/OT; ensure safe dosing and monitoring plan.
Explain diagnosis, typical course, and that diagnosis is clinical with exclusion of mimics; discuss genetics/family history relevance.
Explain treatment goals, gradual titration, common side effects (sedation/dizziness/weakness, fall risk), and warn not to stop baclofen abruptly due to withdrawal risk.
Lifestyle and safety counseling: daily stretching/exercise, fall prevention, assistive devices, and avoiding excess alcohol/sedatives.
Arrange follow-up to assess response/side effects, titrate therapy, review test results, and consider genetics referral; provide return precautions for red flags.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and safety-net instructions.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 22-year-old woman. You are vague and a bit evasive, giving short answers unless asked directly. Start with: “I can’t stop feeling hungry, and I keep gaining weight no matter what I do.” Also volunteer: you’ve been overweight since childhood, routines changing can trigger big meltdowns, and school (reading/math) has always been hard despite trying.

If asked about eating, admit you sometimes eat in secret because you feel desperate and don’t feel full. If asked about sleep/energy, mention daytime sleepiness, loud snoring, and pauses in breathing. If asked, mention constipation, low muscle strength, skin picking when stressed, and anxiety/ruminating thoughts.

Do NOT mention reduced pain/temperature sensing, low thirst, overheating/low sweating, binge episodes, or small hands/feet unless specifically asked.

Resist admitting: taking food from others/trash, inappropriate self-touching when stressed, and occasional nighttime urine accidents—deny at first, then reluctantly acknowledge if the clinician is nonjudgmental.

Express misconceptions: “My thyroid is broken” and “maybe it’s Cushing’s,” and say you take OTC “metabolism booster” pills. Worry about surprise bills and assume healthy plans are unaffordable. If met with empathy, soften, share a bit more, but remain embarrassed.

Genetic testing - Prader-Willi methylati.value: Normal methylation pattern (no deletion/UPD/imprinting defect detected)

Genetic testing - MAGEL2 sequencing.value: Pathogenic heterozygous truncating variant consistent with Schaaf-Yang syndrome (Prader-Willi-like due to point mutation)

Genetic testing - CEP19.value: Biallelic pathogenic variants consistent with CEP19-related morbid obesity

confounders: Hypothyroidism symptoms overlap (fatigue/weight gain) despite currently normal thyroid labs
Depression/anxiety can mimic or worsen cognitive/learning difficulties and contribute to overeating
Obstructive sleep apnea risk from severe obesity may cause daytime somnolence and impaired attention

required_evidence: Documented pathogenic point mutation consistent with a Prader-Willi-like phenotype (e.g., in genes within/related to the PWS critical region or PWS-like syndromic obesity genes) and clinical correlation
Neurodevelopmental phenotype including specific learning disability
Exclude classic Prader-Willi mechanisms if not already done (DNA methylation testing for 15q11-q13; deletion/UPD/imprinting center defects)
Assess for common metabolic complications of PWS/PWS-like states (hyperphagia/obesity risk, insulin resistance/diabetes, dyslipidemia, fatty liver)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Often normal to mildly elevated; may be elevated if insulin resistance or type 2 diabetes is present (screening target <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7–6.4%; diabetes ≥6.5%)
expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Often normal to mildly elevated; impaired fasting glucose 100–125 mg/dL possible; diabetes ≥126 mg/dL if present
expected_lab_results.Fasting insulin: Often elevated relative to glucose (suggesting insulin resistance), though can be normal if lean or early disease
expected_lab_results.Lipid panel - LDL cholesterol (calculate: May be normal or elevated depending on diet/weight and family risk
expected_lab_results.ALT: May be mildly elevated if metabolic dysfunction–associated steatotic liver disease (fatty liver) is present
expected_lab_results.AST: May be mildly elevated; typically less than ALT if fatty liver

medications: Metformin 500 mg PO once daily with food, increase by 500 mg every 1-2 weeks as tolerated to 1000 mg PO twice daily (if prediabetes/type 2 diabetes or significant insulin resistance with obesity)
Semaglutide 0.25 mg SC weekly x 4 weeks, then 0.5 mg SC weekly; titrate per response/tolerability (if obesity with metabolic complications and age/indication appropriate)
Atorvastatin 10-20 mg PO nightly (if LDL-C elevated or overall ASCVD risk warrants per age-specific guidelines)
Omega-3 ethyl esters 2 g PO twice daily OR fenofibrate 145 mg PO daily (if severe hypertriglyceridemia, e.g., ≥500 mg/dL, to reduce pancreatitis risk)
non_pharmacological: Structured environmental food security plan (locked/controlled access to food, consistent meal schedule, supervision as needed) and behavioral support
Dietitian-led calorie-controlled, nutrient-dense diet; limit sugar-sweetened beverages and refined carbohydrates; adequate protein and fiber
Physical activity plan tailored to ability: goal ≥150 min/week moderate activity plus resistance training 2 days/week as feasible
Screen and manage comorbidities common in PWS/PWS-like phenotypes: sleep-disordered breathing (sleep study if symptoms), hypertension, scoliosis, hypogonadism, and mental/behavioral health needs
Neuropsychological/educational supports for specific learning disability (IEP/504 plan, speech/occupational therapy as indicated)
Liver health counseling (weight management, avoid alcohol/hepatotoxins) and consider hepatic ultrasound if persistent ALT/AST elevation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck weight/BMI, blood pressure, and medication tolerance in 4-6 weeks; repeat A1c and fasting lipids in 3 months (earlier if medication titration or severe abnormalities); repeat ALT/AST in 3 months if elevated.

nl_assertions: Agent explains that congenital factor XIII deficiency is an inherited clotting factor problem that can cause delayed bleeding even when PT/aPTT and other routine clotting tests are normal.
Agent clarifies that CBC/iron studies can show anemia/iron deficiency from bleeding but do not measure factor XIII activity; avoids conflating anemia labs with diagnosis confirmation.

Agent elicits and documents bleeding phenotype and red flags: intracranial bleed symptoms (head injury, severe headache, neurologic symptoms), uncontrolled bleeding, large expanding hematomas, heavy menstrual bleeding, post-procedural bleeding.
Agent recommends factor XIII replacement as main therapy and states prophylaxis reduces spontaneous and life-threatening bleeding risk.

Agent describes how factor is given (IV), typical prophylaxis cadence (about every 4 weeks, product-dependent), and that extra dosing is needed for active bleeds and before/after procedures; encourages hematology coordination rather than giving unsafe fixed peri-procedural dosing without context.

Agent discusses risks of factor XIII products: rare thrombosis and hypersensitivity/infusion reactions; provides clear instructions to stop infusion and seek urgent care for anaphylaxis symptoms (hives, wheeze, swelling, hypotension) or concerning thrombotic symptoms.
Agent addresses procedure planning: advises contacting hematology in advance for surgeries/dental work and provides an emergency plan/medical alert recommendation.

Agent provides medication safety counseling: avoid aspirin/NSAIDs unless specifically approved; discusses acetaminophen as a safer analgesic alternative when appropriate; asks about and reviews new meds/supplements.

Agent performs critical safety checks before prescribing: allergy check (penicillin causes hives) and contraindication check (prior reactions, thrombosis history/risk); does not prescribe prior to completing these checks.
Agent performs drug interaction review before prescribing (non-critical) and documents rationale even if no interactions found.
Agent uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and provides safety-net instructions for urgent evaluation when red flags occur.

Agent avoids inappropriate therapies (e.g., warfarin/DOACs for bleeding, routine use of antifibrinolytics without considering contraindications) and avoids minimizing risk of intracranial hemorrhage in factor XIII deficiency.

thrombosis history or major risk factors (prior VTE, strong family history, active cancer, prolonged immobility, estrogen therapy)
